did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used trained convolutional neural networks for AI-based segmentation of high-frequency ultrasound images to automatically identify and color-code skin layers (epidermis, dermis, subcutis) and pathological features (tumors, nodules, fluid-filled cavities) to improve image interpretability and diagnostic consistency.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and describes a clinical study involving the application of dermoscopy-guided high-frequency ultrasound to 130 lesions from 122 patients across three centers. It presents original experimental data, technical capabilities, and performance evaluation of a specific imaging device, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Dermoscopy-guided high-frequency ultrasound: Principles and applications in dermatology
brief_summary: This study evaluated the design, technical capabilities, and clinical application of dermoscopy-guided high-frequency ultrasound (DG-HFUS), a portable multimodal imaging device that integrates surface dermoscopy with subsurface ultrasound visualization. The device was applied to 130 lesions from 122 patients across three international centers to assess its performance in diagnosing malignant skin cancers and inflammatory disorders. Trained convolutional neural networks were employed for AI-based segmentation to automatically identify and color-code skin layers and pathological features, thereby improving image interpretability and diagnostic consistency. The DG-HFUS system successfully enabled real-time correlation of surface dermoscopic patterns with underlying structural alterations, identifying disease-specific features for both malignant and inflammatory conditions. The findings suggest that DG-HFUS bridges the gap between surface and subsurface imaging, offering a practical, cost-effective solution for noninvasive dermatologic diagnosis at the point of care.

lesion_types_studied.details: 40 lesions studied; 37 of 40 (92.5%) appeared as well-defined hypoechoic lesions with internal heterogeneity in 80.0% of cases.

15 lesions studied; 13 of 15 (86.7%) appeared as hypoechoic areas; acoustic shadowing was present in 73.3% of cases.
Inflammatory condition; DG-HFUS identified inflammatory hypoechoic upper dermal band in 100% of lesional areas.

Used as baseline for comparison; imaging delineated normal layers (epidermis, dermis, subcutis) and hair follicles.

all_task_types.target: Skin layers (epidermis, dermis, subcutis) and pathological features (tumors, nodules, fluid-filled cavities)
all_task_types.details: Trained convolutional neural networks were used for AI-based segmentation to automatically identify and color-code specific anatomical layers and pathological abnormalities in high-frequency ultrasound images to improve interpretability.

The device was evaluated for its ability to estimate tumor depth, achieving a sensitivity of 91.8% and specificity of 96.0%, with a Pearson correlation coefficient of r = 0.943 (P < .0001) compared to histopathology.

all_task_types.target: High-risk vs low-risk Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) subtypes
all_task_types.details: DG-HFUS demonstrated the ability to distinguish between low- and high-risk BCC subtypes based on lesion morphology, achieving 83.33% sensitivity and 91.66% specificity in a cohort of 63 BCCs.

datasets_used.name: DG-HFUS Clinical Cohort
datasets_used.description: A proprietary, multi-center clinical dataset of in vivo dermoscopy-guided high-frequency ultrasound (DG-HFUS) images and corresponding dermoscopy images collected from 122 patients across three international centers: Semmelweis University (Budapest, Hungary), Universite Libre de Bruxelles (Brussels, Belgium), and Hospital Clinic, Universidad de Barcelona (Barcelona, Spain). The study population had a mean age of 56 ± 16.2 years (54% female, 46% male). Data collection occurred between November 2, 2021, and January 21, 2025. No public dataset is available; data and analyses are available on reasonable request to the corresponding author.

use.training: The trained convolutional neural networks for AI-based segmentation were developed to automatically identify and color-code skin layers and pathological features; however, the specific split of this cohort for training the AI model is not reported.
use.validation: Histopathologic biopsy served as the gold standard for skin cancer diagnoses to validate the imaging findings. For inflammatory conditions, performance was assessed using dermatologic clinical examinations and validated clinical scoring systems.
use.testing: The DG-HFUS system's performance was evaluated on this cohort to assess its ability to identify disease-specific imaging features for malignant and inflammatory lesions, correlate surface dermoscopic patterns with subsurface structures, and estimate tumor depth.
use.other: Adjacent healthy skin from 1 patient (imaged within surgical safety margins during BCC resection) was scanned in vivo and analyzed ex vivo to validate the system's ability to detect and coregister normal anatomical features.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: PASI-inflammatory hypoechoic upper dermal band correlations

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the correlation between DG-HFUS estimated tumor depth and histopathology measurements.
To assess the diagnostic performance of DG-HFUS for distinguishing high-risk vs low-risk Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) subtypes and for estimating tumor depth.
To report the frequency of specific imaging features (e.g., hypoechoic appearance, acoustic shadowing, irregular thickness) across different lesion types (BCC, Melanoma, SCC, HS, Psoriasis).

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no linear correlation between DG-HFUS estimated tumor depth and histopathology measurements.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant linear correlation between DG-HFUS estimated tumor depth and histopathology measurements.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study reported a Pearson correlation coefficient of r = 0.943 with a p-value of P < .0001, indicating a statistically significant strong positive correlation between the ultrasound-estimated tumor depth and the histopathological gold standard.

Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) (n=40).notes: 37 of 40 (92.5%) appeared as well-defined hypoechoic lesions with internal heterogeneity in 80.0% of cases. DG-HFUS distinguished between low- and high-risk BCC subtypes with 83.33% sensitivity and 91.66% specificity in a cohort of 63 BCCs (note: text specifies 40 BCCs in cohort but 63 in classification context, likely referring to the specific subset used for this metric).

Melanoma (n=40).notes: 27 of 40 (67.5%) appeared as hypoechoic lesions extending into surrounding tissue, with irregular thickness noted in 62.5%.

Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) (n=15).notes: 13 of 15 (86.7%) appeared as hypoechoic areas; acoustic shadowing was present in 73.3% of cases.

Psoriasis (n=10 lesions from 9 patients).notes: DG-HFUS identified inflammatory hypoechoic upper dermal band in 100% of lesional areas.

DG-HFUS.normal layers delineation: Epidermis, dermis, subcutis, and hair follicles visible

Tumor Depth Estimation (Sub-analysis).notes: The device was evaluated for its ability to estimate tumor depth, achieving a sensitivity of 91.8% and specificity of 96.0%, with a Pearson correlation coefficient of r = 0.943 (P < .0001) compared to histopathology.

architecture_details.description: Trained convolutional neural networks used for AI-based segmentation to automatically identify and color-code skin layers (epidermis, dermis, subcutis) and pathological features (tumors, nodules, fluid-filled cavities).

preprocessing_techniques: AI-based segmentation using trained convolutional neural networks to automatically identify and color-code skin layers (epidermis, dermis, subcutis) and pathological features (tumors, nodules, fluid-filled cavities)
Coregistration of dermoscopy images with high-frequency ultrasound (HFUS) images to align surface localization with subsurface visualization
Exclusion of lesions on bleeding, purulent, or compromised skin surfaces to ensure imaging accuracy
Exclusion of lesions in anatomically challenging regions (eyelids, eyebrows, ears, nasal folds) where probe placement was hindered by curvature
conclusions: Dermoscopy-guided high-frequency ultrasound (DG-HFUS) enables simultaneous visualization and correlation of surface dermoscopic patterns with underlying subsurface structural alterations in real time.

The DG-HFUS device successfully identified disease-specific imaging features for both malignant skin cancers (basal cell carcinoma, melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma) and inflammatory disorders (psoriasis, hidradenitis suppurativa).

Artificial intelligence-based segmentation using trained convolutional neural networks improved image interpretability by automatically identifying and color-coding skin layers and pathological features, thereby reducing interobserver variability.

DG-HFUS bridges the diagnostic gap between surface and subsurface dermatologic imaging, offering a practical, portable, and cost-effective solution for noninvasive diagnosis and management at the point of care.

The study acknowledges that DG-HFUS should not be viewed as a replacement for high-end modalities like histology or optical coherence tomography in applications requiring microscopic resolution, but rather as a complementary tool for rapid, real-time assessment.
important_remarks: Potential conflict of interest: Two authors (MG and KF) are employees of Dermus, the manufacturer of the DG-HFUS device used in the study, although they claim no role in study design or data interpretation.
Limited external validation: The study was conducted at only three international centers (Hungary, Belgium, Spain), limiting generalizability to other populations or healthcare settings.

Small sample size for specific conditions: The cohort included only 10 psoriasis lesions, 15 hidradenitis suppurativa lesions, and 1 healthy skin sample, which may limit statistical power for these inflammatory conditions.

Exclusion criteria bias: Lesions on bleeding, purulent, or compromised skin, as well as those in anatomically challenging regions (eyelids, ears, nasal folds), were excluded, potentially limiting applicability to complex clinical presentations.
Lack of public dataset: No public dataset is available; data and analyses are available only on reasonable request to the corresponding author, hindering independent replication.

AI model details not reported: While trained convolutional neural networks were used for segmentation, specific architecture details, training data, loss functions, and hyperparameters are not reported.

Discrepancy in BCC cohort size for subtype classification: The text mentions a cohort of 63 BCCs for high-risk vs low-risk subtype classification, while the total BCC count in the main cohort is listed as 40, creating ambiguity about the subset used.

Device limitations acknowledged: The authors note that DG-HFUS should not replace high-end modalities like histology or OCT where microscopic resolution is required, positioning it as a complementary tool.
